questions.specification:
A city planner is analyzing the function representing the cost of public transportation, given by
$h(x) = \sqrt{x + 1}$

This function is transformed to produce various other functions.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Functions > AHL 2.8—Transformations of graphs, composite transformations > Question Type 3: Given a description of transformations, finding the appropriate transformed function
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Given a description of transformations, finding the appropriate transformed function
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts b–d ask for equations after reflections in the x- and y-axis and to describe the combined transformations to obtain f(x)=-sqrt(-x+1), i.e., determining transformed functions from described transformations.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Functions > AHL 2.8—Transformations of graphs, composite transformations > Question Type 4: Creating a description explaining function transformations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Creating a description explaining function transformations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part d explicitly asks to describe multiple transformations (reflection in both axes and a horizontal shift) applied to h(x) to obtain the given f(x).

parts.content: Write down the equation of $j(x)$ if it represents $h(x)$ reflected in the $x$-axis.
parts.markscheme: $j(x) = -\sqrt{x + 1}$ <marks>A2</marks>

steps.title: Identify the original function
steps.content:
To start, we identify the original function given in the problem, which represents the cost of public transportation:

$$h(x) = \sqrt{x + 1}$$

This function describes the initial cost model before any changes are made.

steps.title: Recall the transformation rule
steps.content:
The question asks for a **reflection in the $x$-axis**. In mathematics, reflecting a graph across the $x$-axis means that every positive $y$-value becomes negative, and every negative $y$-value becomes positive.

This transformation is covered in **Topic 2: Functions** (specifically section AHL 2.8). The algebraic rule for this transformation is:

$$j(x) = -h(x)$$ 

This means we need to negate the entire output of the function.

steps.title: Apply the negation
steps.content:
Now, we apply the rule by placing a negative sign in front of the entire expression for $h(x)$:

$$\begin{aligned} j(x) &= -(h(x)) \\ j(x) &= -(\sqrt{x + 1}) \end{aligned}$$

By simply negating the square root, we have successfully transformed the function to represent its reflection.

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The final equation for the transformed function $j(x)$ is:

$$j(x) = -\sqrt{x + 1}$$

In the IB markscheme, clearly stating this final form would earn you full marks.

steps.title: Define the starting function
steps.content: We are given the function $h(x) = \sqrt{x + 1}$. Our goal is to find the equation for $j(x)$, which is a reflection of this function across the $x$-axis.

steps.title: Visualize on a GDC
steps.content:
To see what this looks like, let's plot the original function on a graphing calculator. 

Notice that for any $x$-value, the graph of $h(x)$ produces a positive $y$-value (or zero). For example, at $x=3$, $h(3) = \sqrt{3+1} = 2$.

gdc.instructions: Open the graph menu and enter the function h(x) = sqrt(x + 1).

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=]. Enter √(X+1) into Y1. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation into the input bar.

calculator.description: Plot the original function h(x).

steps.title: Identify the transformation
steps.content:
A reflection in the $x$-axis flips the graph vertically. This means that every point $(x, y)$ on the original graph moves to $(x, -y)$. 

Using your GDC, you can test this by plotting a second function where you multiply the original by $-1$. You will see the curve 'mirror' itself below the $x$-axis.

gdc.instructions: Enter a second function y2 = -y1 to see the reflection.

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=]. Enter -Y1 into Y2 (use [VARS] -> [Y-VARS] -> [Function] -> [Y1]). Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Add a second line to compare the graphs.

calculator.description: Plot the reflected function to verify.

steps.title: Write the final equation
steps.content:
In general, a reflection in the $x$-axis of any function $f(x)$ is represented by $-f(x)$. Since our function is $h(x) = \sqrt{x + 1}$, we simply place a negative sign in front of the entire expression:

$$j(x) = -\sqrt{x + 1}$$

This accounts for 2 marks in the markscheme for correctly identifying and applying the negative sign to the function.

steps.title: Identify the original function
steps.content:
We start with the function representing the cost of transportation:

$$h(x) = \sqrt{x + 1}$$

Our goal is to find the equation for $j(x)$, which is a transformation of this original function.

steps.title: Apply coordinate mapping rule
steps.content:
When a graph is **reflected in the $x$-axis**, every point $(x, y)$ on the original graph is mapped to a new point $(x, -y)$. 

This means that for every input $x$, the output (the $y$-value) is multiplied by $-1$.

steps.title: Create the new function
steps.content:
Since the $y$-value of our original function is $h(x)$, the new function $j(x)$ must be equal to the negative of $h(x)$:

$$j(x) = -h(x)$$

Substituting our specific function for $h(x)$, we get:

$$j(x) = -(\sqrt{x + 1})$$

steps.title: Finalize the equation
steps.content:
The final equation for the transformed function is:

$$j(x) = -\sqrt{x + 1}$$

In the IB markscheme, simply writing this correct equation earns you both marks.

parts.content: Write down the equation of $g(x)$ if it represents $h(x)$ reflected in the $y$-axis.
parts.markscheme: $g(x) = \sqrt{-x + 1}$ (or $g(x) = \sqrt{1 - x}$) <marks>A2</marks>

parts.content: Describe the transformations applied to $h(x)$ to obtain $f(x) = -\sqrt{-x + 1}$.
parts.markscheme:
reflection in the $y$-axis <marks>A1</marks>
reflection in the $x$-axis <marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** Accept "reflection in the origin" for <marks>A2</marks>. The order of the reflections does not matter.

steps.title: Analyze the input change
steps.content:
To find the transformations, we first look at the changes made to the variable $x$ inside the function's core operation (the square root). 

Comparing the original function $h(x) = \sqrt{x + 1}$ to the transformed function $f(x) = -\sqrt{-x + 1}$, we see that $x$ has been replaced with $-x$ inside the square root. 

In transformation theory, substituting $x$ with $-x$ results in a **reflection in the $y$-axis** (a horizontal reflection).

steps.title: Analyze the output change
steps.content:
Next, we look at the changes made outside the square root, which affect the output (the $y$-values).

Notice the negative sign in front of the entire square root in $f(x) = -\sqrt{-x + 1}$. Since the original function $h(x)$ produced positive square root values, placing a negative sign outside means every output value is multiplied by $-1$.

Multiplying the entire function by $-1$ results in a **reflection in the $x$-axis** (a vertical reflection).

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: We can verify these reflections by graphing both functions on a calculator. You will see that the graph of $f(x)$ is the graph of $h(x)$ flipped both vertically and horizontally.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graph menu.
2. Input f1(x) = sqrt(x + 1).
3. Input f2(x) = -sqrt(-x + 1).
4. Draw the graphs.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = √(X + 1).
3. Enter Y2 = -√(-X + 1).
4. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions:
1. Type h(x) = sqrt(x + 1).
2. Type f(x) = -sqrt(-x + 1).

calculator.description: Graph both functions to visualize the reflections.

steps.title: Summarize the transformations
steps.content:
Based on our analysis of the input and output changes, the two transformations applied to $h(x)$ are:

1.  **Reflection in the $y$-axis** (due to the $-x$)
2.  **Reflection in the $x$-axis** (due to the negative sign outside the root)

Note: In the IB markscheme, mention of both these individual reflections earns the full 2 marks. Mentioning a "reflection in the origin" is also an acceptable way to describe these two reflections combined.

steps.title: Compare function structures
steps.content:
To describe the transformations, let's look at how the original function $h(x)$ was changed to create $f(x)$.

Starting with:
$$h(x) = \sqrt{x + 1}$$

And the target function:
$$f(x) = -\sqrt{-x + 1}$$

Notice that the variable $x$ inside the square root has changed to $-x$, and the entire function has been multiplied by $-1$.

steps.title: Relate f(x) to h(x)
steps.content:
We can express $f(x)$ directly in terms of $h(x)$ by performing the substitutions manually:

1. Replace $x$ with $-x$:
$$h(-x) = \sqrt{-x + 1}$$

2. Multiply the result by $-1$:
$$-h(-x) = -\sqrt{-x + 1}$$

This shows that $$f(x) = -h(-x)$$.

steps.title: Identify composite transformations
steps.content:
According to **Topic 2: Functions** (AHL 2.8), we can interpret these changes as individual reflections:

*   Replacing $x$ with $-x$ is a **reflection in the $y$-axis**.
*   Multiplying the function by $-1$ is a **reflection in the $x$-axis**.

Performing both a horizontal reflection and a vertical reflection results in a single point transformation.

steps.title: State the final transformation
steps.content:
When a function undergoes the transformation $f(x) = -h(-x)$, it is equivalent to a single point reflection through the center of the coordinate system.

Therefore, the transformation is a **reflection in the origin**.

highlights.text: Accept "reflection in the origin" for A2

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Given a description of transformations, finding the appropriate transformed function
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The task is to find the image of a point on y=f(x) under the transformed function y=2f(x-3)+4, involving horizontal shift, vertical scaling, and vertical translation.

parts.content: Find the coordinates of the corresponding point under $y=2 f(x-3)+4$.
parts.markscheme:
- Apply the horizontal translation: $x = -1 + 3$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the new x-coordinate: $x = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Apply the vertical transformations: $y = 2(3) + 4$ <marks>M1</marks>
- State the final coordinates: $(2, 10)$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the transformation
steps.content:
To find the new coordinates using the component-wise approach, we look at the horizontal and vertical changes separately. We are starting with the point $A(-1, 3)$ on the curve $y=f(x)$ and moving to the new curve:

$$y = 2 f(x-3) + 4$$

This transformation involves:
1.  A horizontal shift (inside the brackets).
2.  A vertical stretch and a vertical shift (outside the brackets).

steps.title: Transform the x-coordinate
steps.content:
Horizontal transformations are found inside the function's argument. In $f(x-3)$, the $-3$ indicates a horizontal translation of $+3$ units to the right.

We apply this to the original x-coordinate:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{new} &= x_{old} + 3 \\ x_{new} &= -1 + 3 \\ x_{new} &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

This step earns you the first two marks on the markscheme.

gdc.instructions: Enter -1 + 3 on the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter -1 + 3 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type -1 + 3 into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the new x-coordinate.

steps.title: Transform the y-coordinate
steps.content:
Vertical transformations are applied to the entire function. In $2f(...) + 4$, we have two steps:
1.  **Vertical Stretch**: Multiply by $2$ (scale factor 2).
2.  **Vertical Translation**: Add $4$ (shift up 4 units).

Applying these to the original y-coordinate ($y=3$):

$$\begin{aligned} y_{new} &= 2(y_{old}) + 4 \\ y_{new} &= 2(3) + 4 \\ y_{new} &= 10 \end{aligned}$$

This calculation provides the third mark for applying the vertical transformations correctly.

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * 3 + 4 on the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 * 3 + 4 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 2(3) + 4 into a calculation cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the new y-coordinate.

steps.title: State final coordinates
steps.content:
Combining our new $x$ and $y$ values, the corresponding point on the new curve is:

$$(2, 10)$$

This completes the final mark for the question.

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content:
We are given that the point $A(-1, 3)$ lies on the curve $y = f(x)$. 

This tells us two important things:
1. When the input is $-1$, the function's value is $3$.
2. Mathematically, we can write this as $f(-1) = 3$.

steps.title: Find the new x-coordinate
steps.content:
To use the functional substitution approach for the new curve $y = 2f(x-3) + 4$, we want the "inner" part of the function (the part inside the parentheses) to equal our known input, which is $-1$.

So, we set the inner expression equal to $-1$:

$$x - 3 = -1$$

Solving for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= -1 + 3 \\ x &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter -1 + 3 on the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter -1 + 3 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type -1 + 3 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Verify the simple addition to find the new x-coordinate.

steps.title: Evaluate the new y-coordinate
steps.content:
Now that we know the new $x$-coordinate is $2$, we substitute this back into the transformation equation to find the corresponding $y$-value:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= 2f(2 - 3) + 4 \\ y &= 2f(-1) + 4 \end{aligned}$$

From Step 0, we know that $f(-1) = 3$. Now we substitute that value in:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= 2(3) + 4 \\ y &= 6 + 4 \\ y &= 10 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * 3 + 4 on the calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 * 3 + 4 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 2 * 3 + 4 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the final y-value after substituting the known function value.

steps.title: State the final coordinates
steps.content:
By pairing our new $x$-coordinate and $y$-coordinate, we find the coordinates of the corresponding point on the transformed curve.

The final coordinates are $(2, 10)$.

questions.specification: A local community is developing a new park and wants to model the height of a parabolic fountain using the quadratic function $f(x) = p + qx - x^2$. The fountain reaches its maximum height of 5 meters when positioned at $x = 3$ meters from the base.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Functions > SL 2.5—Modelling functions > Question Type 4: Calculating the vertex and axis of symmetry of quadratic functions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Calculating the vertex and axis of symmetry of quadratic functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) uses the maximum height at x=3 for a quadratic to determine parameters p and q via vertex/turning-point conditions (axis of symmetry/vertex value).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Functions > AHL 2.8—Transformations of graphs, composite transformations > Question Type 3: Given a description of transformations, finding the appropriate transformed function
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Given a description of transformations, finding the appropriate transformed function
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for shifting the parabola 3 meters to the right, which is a horizontal translation requiring forming the transformed function f(x-3).

parts.content: Determine the values of $p$ and $q$ that define this fountain's height.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $x = 3$ and $f(3) = 5$ into the function: $p + 3q - 3^2 = 5 \Rightarrow p + 3q = 14$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Find the derivative of $f(x)$ and set it to zero at $x = 3$ to represent the maximum height: $f'(x) = q - 2x \Rightarrow q - 2(3) = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $q$: $q = 6$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Substitute $q = 6$ into the first equation to find $p$: $p + 3(6) = 14 \Rightarrow p = -4$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: If the fountain's design is modified to shift it 3 meters to the right, calculate the equation of the new height function.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute the values $p = -4$ and $q = 6$ into the original function: $f(x) = -x^2 + 6x - 4$
- Substitute $(x - 3)$ for $x$ in the function to shift it 3 units to the right: $g(x) = -(x - 3)^2 + 6(x - 3) - 4$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Expand the binomial and simplify the expression:
$$\begin{aligned} g(x) &= -(x^2 - 6x + 9) + 6x - 18 - 4 \\ &= -x^2 + 6x - 9 + 6x - 22 \end{aligned}$$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Conclude the new height function: $g(x) = -x^2 + 12x - 31$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Find the coefficient q
steps.content:
The original function is $f(x) = -x^2 + qx + p$. We know the maximum height (the vertex) occurs at $x = 3$. We can use the axis of symmetry formula from the data booklet:

$$
x = -\frac{b}{2a}
$$

Substituting $a = -1$, $b = q$, and $x = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
3 &= -\frac{q}{2(-1)} \\
3 &= \frac{q}{2} \\
q &= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

This aligns with Topic 2.5 on quadratic modeling.

highlights.text: maximum height of 5 meters when positioned at x = 3 meters

steps.title: Find the coefficient p
steps.content:
Now we use the fact that the fountain reaches a height of 5 meters when $x = 3$. We substitute $x = 3$, $f(x) = 5$, and $q = 6$ into the function equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
5 &= p + 6(3) - (3)^2 \\
5 &= p + 18 - 9 \\
5 &= p + 9 \\
p &= -4
\end{aligned}
$$

So, the original height function is $f(x) = -x^2 + 6x - 4$.

highlights.text: maximum height of 5 meters when positioned at x = 3 meters

steps.title: Apply the horizontal shift
steps.content:
To shift the function 3 meters to the right, we replace every $x$ in the original function with $(x - 3)$. This follows the transformation rule $g(x) = f(x - h)$ for a horizontal shift of $h$ units.

$$g(x) = -(x - 3)^2 + 6(x - 3) - 4$$

highlights.text: shift it 3 meters to the right

steps.title: Expand and simplify
steps.content:
We now expand the terms to find the final equation for $g(x)$:

1. Expand the squared binomial: $(x - 3)^2 = x^2 - 6x + 9$
2. Distribute the negative sign: $-(x^2 - 6x + 9) = -x^2 + 6x - 9$
3. Distribute the 6: $6(x - 3) = 6x - 18$

Combine all parts:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(x) &= (-x^2 + 6x - 9) + (6x - 18) - 4 \\
g(x) &= -x^2 + (6x + 6x) + (-9 - 18 - 4) \\
g(x) &= -x^2 + 12x - 31
\end{aligned}
$$

This final expression represents the height function after the modification.

steps.title: Verify using technology

steps.title: Identify the original vertex
steps.content:
To use the **Vertex Translation Method**, we first need the coordinates of the vertex $(h, k)$ of the original function. The question states that the fountain reaches a maximum height at a specific position.

From the text, we see the maximum height is $5$ meters and it occurs at $x = 3$. This gives us the vertex:
$$(h, k) = (3, 5)$$ 

This represents the peak of the parabolic fountain.

steps.title: Write original vertex form
steps.content:
The vertex form of a quadratic is given by:
$$f(x) = a(x - h)^2 + k$$

Looking at the original equation $f(x) = p + qx - x^2$, the coefficient of the $x^2$ term (which is $a$) is $-1$. Using our vertex $(3, 5)$, we can write the original function as:
$$f(x) = -1(x - 3)^2 + 5$$

steps.title: Translate the vertex
steps.content:
The design is modified to shift the fountain 3 meters to the right. In the vertex translation method, we simply update the $x$-coordinate of the vertex.

Original vertex: $(3, 5)$
Shift: $+3$ units horizontally
New vertex: $(3 + 3, 5) = (6, 5)$

Our new function $g(x)$ in vertex form is:
$$g(x) = -(x - 6)^2 + 5$$

highlights.text: shift it 3 meters to the right

steps.title: Expand to standard form
steps.content:
To get the final equation, we expand the squared binomial and simplify:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g(x) &= -(x - 6)^2 + 5 \\
g(x) &= -(x^2 - 12x + 36) + 5 \\
g(x) &= -x^2 + 12x - 36 + 5 \\
g(x) &= -x^2 + 12x - 31
\end{aligned}
$$

This matches the simplified form required by the markscheme.

$$g(x) = -x^2 + 12x - 31$$

parts.content: Write the equation of the function after a horizontal shrink of $f(x)$ by a factor of 3.
parts.markscheme: $y = \ln(3x)$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the function and transformation
steps.content:
In this problem, we are starting with the natural logarithm function, $f(x) = \ln(x)$. We need to apply a **horizontal shrink by a factor of 3**.

This concept is covered in **Topic 2: Functions** (specifically **AHL 2.8**), which deals with how changing the input or output of a function affects its graph.

highlights.text: horizontal shrink of f(x) by a factor of 3

steps.title: Recall the transformation rule
steps.content:
For any function $f(x)$, a horizontal stretch or shrink by a scale factor $k$ is represented by the transformation:

$$y = f\left(\frac{1}{k}x\right)$$

When we are told there is a **shrink by a factor of 3**, this means the scale factor $k$ is equal to $\frac{1}{3}$.

steps.title: Apply the rule to the function
steps.content:
Now we substitute our scale factor $k = \frac{1}{3}$ into the transformation formula:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= f\left(\frac{1}{1/3}x\right) \\ y &= f(3x) \end{aligned}$$

Since our original function is $f(x) = \ln(x)$, we replace the $x$ inside the logarithm with $3x$.

gdc.instructions: Graph y = ln(x) and y = ln(3x) to see that for every y-value, the corresponding x-value in the second graph is 1/3 of the original.

3. Enter Y2 = ln(3X).
4. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type y = ln(x) into the first expression box and y = ln(3x) into the second.

calculator.description: You can verify the transformation by graphing both functions on your GDC. The graph of ln(3x) should appear 'squashed' horizontally toward the y-axis compared to ln(x).

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The final equation after the horizontal shrink is:

$$y = \ln(3x)$$

This matches the requirement for a 1-mark question where stating the correct transformed expression earns the full mark.

steps.title: Define the coordinate mapping
steps.content:
To perform a horizontal transformation, we look at how the coordinates of an original point $(x, y)$ on the graph of $f(x) = \ln(x)$ map to a new point $(x', y')$. 

A **horizontal shrink by a factor of 3** means that the new $x$-coordinates will be $\frac{1}{3}$ of the original $x$-coordinates, while the $y$-coordinates remain the same.

$$\begin{aligned} x' &= \frac{x}{3} \\ y' &= y \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: horizontal shrink of f(x) by a factor of 3

steps.title: Solve for original variables
steps.content:
To find the new equation, we need to express the original variables ($x$ and $y$) in terms of the new ones ($x'$ and $y'$). This allows us to substitute them back into our original function.

From our mapping equations:
$$\begin{aligned} x' = \frac{x}{3} &\Rightarrow x = 3x' \\ y' = y &\Rightarrow y = y' \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute into the function
steps.content:
Now, we take the original function $y = \ln(x)$ and substitute our expressions for $x$ and $y$:

$$y' = \ln(3x')$$

In standard function notation, we drop the primes to write the final equation.

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The transformed function is:

$$y = \ln(3x)$$ 

This matches the expected markscheme where a horizontal shrink by factor $k$ results in the transformation $f(kx)$.

steps.title: Find the original vertex
steps.content:
To use the vertex point translation method, we first need to find the vertex of the original function $f(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 11$. We can find the $x$-coordinate of the vertex using the axis of symmetry formula from your data booklet:

$$x = -\frac{b}{2a}$$

For our function, $a = 3$ and $b = -12$. Substituting these in:

$$x = -\left(\frac{-12}{2(3)}\right) = \frac{12}{6} = 2$$

Now, we find the $y$-coordinate by substituting $x = 2$ back into $f(x)$:

$$\begin{aligned} f(2) &= 3(2)^2 - 12(2) + 11 \\ &= 3(4) - 24 + 11 \\ &= 12 - 24 + 11 \\ &= -1 \end{aligned}$$

So, the original vertex is $(2, -1)$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 3*(2)^2 - 12*(2) + 11 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3*(2)^2 - 12*(2) + 11 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type 3(2)^2 - 12(2) + 11 into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the y-coordinate of the vertex.

steps.title: Translate the vertex point
steps.content:
The question states the graph is translated 3 units in the positive $x$-direction and 5 units in the positive $y$-direction. We apply these changes directly to the coordinates of our vertex $(2, -1)$:

- **New $x$-coordinate ($p$):** $2 + 3 = 5$
- **New $y$-coordinate ($q$):** $-1 + 5 = 4$

The new vertex for function $g$ is $(5, 4)$. This step earns the first mark (M1) for correctly identifying the new vertex.

highlights.text: translated 3 units in the positive x-direction and 5 units in the positive y-direction

steps.title: State the new function
steps.content:
Now we substitute our new vertex $(p, q) = (5, 4)$ into the required vertex form $g(x) = 3(x - p)^2 + q$. Note that the lead coefficient $a = 3$ remains the same as the original function because translation does not change the shape or steepness of the parabola.

$$g(x) = 3(x - 5)^2 + 4$$

This final answer earns the second mark (A1).

steps.title: Identify transformation rules
steps.content:
To find the function $g(x)$, we apply the translation rules to $f(x)$. A translation of $3$ units in the positive $x$-direction and $5$ units in the positive $y$-direction is represented by the functional transformation:

$$g(x) = f(x - 3) + 5$$

highlights.text: translated 3 units in the positive x-direction

steps.title: Prepare the original function
steps.content:
The question asks for the final answer in the form $g(x) = 3(x - p)^2 + q$. It is much easier to apply transformations if we first write $f(x)$ in its own vertex form. 

Using the formula for the axis of symmetry from the data booklet:

$$x = -\frac{b}{2a}$$

For $f(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 11$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= -\left(\frac{-12}{2(3)}\right) \\ x &= 2 \end{aligned}$$

Substituting $x=2$ back into $f(x)$ to find the $y$-coordinate:

$$f(2) = 3(2)^2 - 12(2) + 11 = -1$$

So, the vertex form of $f(x)$ is $f(x) = 3(x - 2)^2 - 1$.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate 3*(2)^2 - 12*(2) + 11 in the calculator menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter 3(2)^2 - 12(2) + 11 on the main screen and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression 3(2)^2 - 12(2) + 11 into a new cell.

calculator.description: Verify the y-coordinate of the vertex for f(x).

steps.title: Apply transformations to vertex
steps.content:
Now we substitute $(x - 3)$ into our vertex form and add $5$:

$$\begin{aligned} g(x) &= f(x - 3) + 5 \\ g(x) &= [3((x - 3) - 2)^2 - 1] + 5 \end{aligned}$$

Simplify the expression inside the parentheses and the constant terms:

$$\begin{aligned} g(x) &= 3(x - 5)^2 - 1 + 5 \\ g(x) &= 3(x - 5)^2 + 4 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Evaluate -1 + 5.

ti84.instructions: Enter -1 + 5 on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type -1 + 5.

calculator.description: Check the constant term -1 + 5.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
We have reached the required form $g(x) = 3(x - p)^2 + q$ where $p = 5$ and $q = 4$.

$$g(x) = 3(x - 5)^2 + 4$$

steps.title: Find the original vertex
steps.content:
To find the function for the translated graph, we first need to identify the vertex (the minimum point) of the original function, $f(x) = 3x^2 - 12x + 11$. 

Using your graphing calculator, you can find that the vertex of the original graph is at $(2, -1)$. This is a great shortcut compared to completing the square by hand!

steps.title: Translate the vertex
steps.content:
Now, we apply the translations given in the problem to the original vertex $(2, -1)$. 

Moving 3 units in the positive $x$-direction means adding 3 to the $x$-coordinate. Moving 5 units in the positive $y$-direction means adding 5 to the $y$-coordinate.

$$\text{New } x = 2 + 3 = 5$$
$$\text{New } y = -1 + 5 = 4$$

The vertex of the new graph, $g(x)$, is at $(5, 4)$. This earns you the first mark!

highlights.text: translated 3 units in the positive x-direction and 5 units in the positive y-direction

steps.title: Write the final function
steps.content:
The question asks for the function in the form $g(x) = 3(x - p)^2 + q$. This is known as vertex form, where $(p, q)$ represents the coordinates of the vertex.

Since the new vertex is $(5, 4)$, we substitute $p = 5$ and $q = 4$ into the formula:

$$g(x) = 3(x - 5)^2 + 4$$

Note that the '3' from the original function stays the same because the translation doesn't change the 'steepness' (vertical stretch) of the parabola.

questions.specification:
Let $f(x) = \frac{x^3}{3} - x^2 - 3x$. Part of the graph of $f$ is shown below.

![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/556fb4d1-9263-4fd5-b9c9-57916690f894.png)

The graph has a maximum point at $A$ and a minimum point at $B(3, -9)$. Write down the coordinates of:

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Functions > AHL 2.8—Transformations of graphs, composite transformations > Question Type 5: Drawing the effect of multiple transformations on a graph
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 5: Drawing the effect of multiple transformations on a graph
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts ask for coordinates after geometric transformations of a point on a graph: reflections in axes, translation by a vector, and a horizontal stretch (a composite transformation).

parts.content: the image of $B$ after reflection in the $y$-axis.
parts.markscheme:
The image of $B$ after reflection in the $y$-axis is $(-3, -9)$.
<marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: the image of $B$ after translation by the vector $\binom{-2}{5}$.
parts.markscheme:
The image of $B$ after translation by the vector $\binom{-2}{5}$ is $(1, -4)$.
<marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: the image of $B$ after reflection in the $x$-axis followed by a horizontal stretch with scale factor $\frac{1}{2}$.
parts.markscheme:
The image of $B$ after reflection in the $x$-axis is $(3, 9)$.
Horizontal stretch with scale factor $\frac{1}{2}$ gives $\left(\frac{3}{2}, 9\right)$ (or $(1.5, 9)$).
<marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the starting point
steps.content: We are starting with the minimum point $B$, which has coordinates $(3, -9)$. We will apply the transformations one by one to these coordinates.

steps.title: Reflect in the x-axis
steps.content:
The first transformation is a **reflection in the $x$-axis**. This transformation flips the graph vertically, which means we change the sign of the $y$-coordinate while keeping the $x$-coordinate the same.

$$(x, y) \rightarrow (x, -y)$$

Applying this to point $B(3, -9)$:

$$\text{New } y = -(-9) = 9$$

The intermediate point is $(3, 9)$.

steps.title: Apply the horizontal stretch
steps.content:
Next, we apply a **horizontal stretch with scale factor $\frac{1}{2}$**. A horizontal stretch multiplies the $x$-coordinate of the point by the scale factor, while the $y$-coordinate remains unchanged.

$$(x, y) \rightarrow (kx, y)$$

Using our intermediate point $(3, 9)$ and the scale factor $k = \frac{1}{2}$:

$$\text{New } x = 3 \times \frac{1}{2} = 1.5$$

The final $y$-coordinate stays $9$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 3 * 0.5.

ti84.instructions: Type 3 * 0.5 and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: In a new cell, type 3 * (1/2).

calculator.description: Calculate the new x-coordinate.

steps.title: State the final image
steps.content:
After both transformations, the coordinates of the image of $B$ are:

$$(1.5, 9) \text{ or } \left(\frac{3}{2}, 9\right)$$ 

This matches the markscheme requirement for the final transformed point.

steps.title: Define the transformed function
steps.content:
To find the coordinates of the image of $B$, we can express the sequence of transformations using function notation. Let $g(x)$ be the function after the transformations are applied to $f(x)$.

1.  **Reflection in the $x$-axis**: This affects the $y$-values by making them negative, represented as $-f(x)$.
2.  **Horizontal stretch with scale factor $\frac{1}{2}$**: In function notation, a horizontal stretch by factor $k$ is represented as $f(\frac{1}{k}x)$. Here, $k = \frac{1}{2}$, so we use $f(2x)$.

Combining these, our transformed function is:
$$g(x) = -f(2x)$$

This tells us that for any point $(x, y)$ on the original graph, we need to find the new $x'$ and $y'$ such that $2x' = x$ and $y' = -y$.

steps.title: Calculate the new x-coordinate
steps.content:
The original $x$-coordinate of point $B$ is $3$. 

In our notation $g(x) = -f(2x)$, the input to the original function $f$ is $2x$. To find the new $x$-coordinate (let's call it $x_{new}$), we set the input equal to the original value:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x_{new} &= 3 \\ x_{new} &= \frac{3}{2} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we can confirm that $\frac{3}{2} = 1.5$.

gdc.instructions: Enter 3 / 2.

ti84.instructions: Press 3 / 2 and Enter.

desmos.instructions: Type 3/2 into a cell.

calculator.description: Verify the division for the x-coordinate.

steps.title: Calculate the new y-coordinate
steps.content:
The original $y$-coordinate of point $B$ is $-9$. 

From our function $g(x) = -f(2x)$, the $y$-value of the image is the negative of the original $y$-value:

$$\begin{aligned} y_{new} &= -(-9) \\ y_{new} &= 9 \end{aligned}$$

This reflects the point across the $x$-axis, changing the sign of the vertical coordinate.

gdc.instructions: Enter -(-9).

ti84.instructions: Press (-) ( (-) 9 ) and Enter.

desmos.instructions: Type -(-9) into a cell.

calculator.description: Calculate the negative of the negative y-value.

steps.title: State the final coordinates
steps.content:
By combining our results, the image of point $B(3, -9)$ is:

$$\left(\frac{3}{2}, 9\right) \text{ or } (1.5, 9)$$

This matches the requirement for a reflection followed by a horizontal compression (stretch by factor $1/2$).

steps.title: Identify the original point
steps.content:
The question provides the coordinates of the minimum point $B$ on the original graph $f(x)$. 

$$B(3, -9)$$ 

We need to find the image of this point after two specific transformations: a reflection in the $x$-axis and a horizontal stretch with scale factor $\frac{1}{2}$.

steps.title: Determine the transformed function
steps.content:
To use the GDC approach, we first determine the algebraic expression for the transformed function, let's call it $g(x)$.

1. **Reflection in the $x$-axis**: This transformation negates the $y$-values, which is represented by $-f(x)$.
2. **Horizontal stretch (SF $\frac{1}{2}$)**: A horizontal stretch by factor $k$ is achieved by replacing $x$ with $\frac{x}{k}$. Here, $x \to \frac{x}{1/2} = 2x$.

Combining these, our new function is:
$$g(x) = -f(2x)$$

Substituting the original expression $f(x) = \frac{x^3}{3} - x^2 - 3x$:
$$g(x) = -\left[ \frac{(2x)^3}{3} - (2x)^2 - 3(2x) \right]$$

This concept is covered in **Topic 2: AHL 2.8—Transformations of graphs**.

steps.title: Analyze the point's nature
steps.content:
Originally, point $B$ was a **minimum**. 
- A reflection in the $x$-axis flips the graph vertically, turning the minimum into a **maximum**.
- A horizontal stretch changes the width but maintains the nature of the turning point.

Therefore, we will look for the **maximum** point on the graph of $g(x)$ in our calculator.

steps.title: Find coordinates using GDC
steps.content: Enter the transformed function into your GDC and use the maximum tool to find the coordinates of the new peak.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graphing menu and enter the function f(x) = -(((2x)^3)/3 - (2x)^2 - 6x).
2. Draw the graph.
3. Use the G-Solv (usually F5) tool and select 'MAX'.

questions.specification:
In a laboratory experiment involving particle physics, a specialized ion source located at a coordinate $(2, 10)$ emits a stream of ions into a vacuum chamber. The trajectory of an individual ion is modeled by the equation $\binom{x}{y} = \binom{2}{10} + t \binom{v_x}{v_y - 3t}$ where $x$ and $y$ represent the horizontal and vertical coordinates in centimetres (cm), and $t$ is the time in microseconds ($μs$) since the ion was emitted.
- $v_x$ is the horizontal component of the initial velocity.
- $v_y$ is the vertical component of the initial velocity.

In this model, the ion and any intercepting beams are treated as single points. For a specific test run, the ion is emitted with an initial velocity such that $v_x = 12$ and $v_y = 18$.

parts.content: Determine the maximum vertical displacement reached by the ion during its flight.
parts.markscheme:
**METHOD 1 - Graphical Method**
- Sketch graph of $y$ against $t$ ($y = 10 + 18t - 3t^2$) <marks>M1</marks>
- Identify maximum height from graph: $y = 37$ cm <marks>A1</marks>

**METHOD 2 - Calculus**
- Differentiate and equate to zero: $\frac{\mathrm{d}y}{\mathrm{d}t} = 18 - 6t = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for $t$: $t = 3$
- Substitute $t=3$ into height equation: $y = 10 + 3(18 - 9) = 37$ cm <marks>A1</marks>

**METHOD 3 - Symmetry/Vertex**
- Identify the time of maximum height using $t = -\frac{b}{2a} = -\frac{18}{2(-3)} = 3$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute $t=3$ to find height: $y = 37$ cm <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: Assuming the ion's path is not interrupted, calculate the $x$-coordinate of the point where the ion would hit a detector screen located on the line $y = 0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to solve $10 + t(18-3t) = 0$ for $t > 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
- $t = \frac{18 + \sqrt{324 + 120}}{6} = 6.511...$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate $x$-coordinate: $x = 2 + 12(6.511...) = 80.1$ cm <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: For the trajectory of the ion, find an expression for $y$ in terms of $x$.
parts.markscheme:
**METHOD 1**
- Express $t$ in terms of $x$: $t = \frac{x-2}{12}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute $t$ into the expression for $y$: $y = 10 + 18\left(\frac{x-2}{12}\right) - 3\left(\frac{x-2}{12}\right)^2$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State simplified expression: $y = 10 + 1.5(x-2) - \frac{1}{48}(x-2)^2$ (or equivalent) <marks>A1</marks>

**METHOD 2**
- Use quadratic form $y = ax^2 + bx + c$ and solve using points $(2, 10)$, $(38, 37)$, and $(80.1, 0)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Find coefficients: $a = -\frac{1}{48}, b = \frac{19}{12}, c = \frac{83}{12}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- State expression: $y = -\frac{1}{48}x^2 + \frac{19}{12}x + \frac{83}{12}$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

parts.content: A sensor captures data along the line $y = 0.5x + 15$. Determine the two coordinates where the ion's path intersects this line.
parts.markscheme:
- Set trajectory equation equal to sensor line: $10 + 1.5(x-2) - \frac{1}{48}(x-2)^2 = 0.5x + 15$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Attempt to solve the resulting quadratic (e.g., $x^2 - 52x + 388 = 0$) <marks>M1</marks>
- State first intersection point: $(9.03, 19.5)$ (or $x = 9.029..., y = 19.514...$) <marks>A1</marks>
- State second intersection point: $(43.0, 36.5)$ (or $x = 42.970..., y = 36.485...$) <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.content: A neutralizing electron beam is emitted from a fixed point $(0, 5)$ and travels in a straight line with a constant speed of $100$ cm $\mu\text{s}^{-1}$. Determine the emission time for the electron beam such that it intercepts the ion at the first intersection point (the one before the ion reaches its maximum vertical displacement).
parts.markscheme:
- Identify target coordinates and calculate time for ion to reach target: $t_{\text{ion}} = \frac{9.029... - 2}{12} = 0.5857...$ $\mu\text{s}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Attempt to find the distance from point $(0, 5)$ to $(9.029..., 19.514...)$: $\sqrt{9.029...^2 + (19.514... - 5)^2} = 17.094...$ cm <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate time for beam to travel this distance: $t_{\text{beam}} = \frac{17.094...}{100} = 0.1709...$ $\mu\text{s}$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Calculate emission time: $t_{\text{emission}} = 0.5857... - 0.1709... = 0.415$ $\mu\text{s}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify the functions
steps.content:
To find the graph of $g(x)$, we first need to express it in its simplest algebraic form. We are given the original function $f(x)$ and the definition for $g(x)$ which involves a transformation of $f(x)$.

This problem relates to **Topic 2: Functions** in your syllabus, specifically working with function values and graphing linear models.

steps.title: Substitute and simplify g(x)
steps.content:
We substitute $(x+3)$ into $f(x)$ everywhere there is an $x$. Then, we subtract $2$ to complete the definition of $g(x)$:

$$\begin{aligned} g(x) &= f(x+3) - 2 \\ g(x) &= [2(x+3) + 1] - 2 \\ g(x) &= 2x + 6 + 1 - 2 \\ g(x) &= 2x + 5 \end{aligned}$$

This simplification earns the first mark on the markscheme.

highlights.text: Substitute the expression for $f(x+3)$ into $g(x)$ and simplify: $g(x) = 2(x+3) + 1 - 2 = 2x + 5$

steps.title: Determine the graph endpoints
steps.content:
The question asks us to draw the graph for a specific domain. To draw a line accurately, we just need to find the coordinates of the two endpoints by plugging the domain limits into our simplified equation:

- At $x = -3$: $g(-3) = 2(-3) + 5 = -1$ $\rightarrow$ point $(-3, -1)$
- At $x = -1$: $g(-1) = 2(-1) + 5 = 3$ $\rightarrow$ point $(-1, 3)$

Ensure your line starts exactly at $x = -3$ and stops exactly at $x = -1$, as this is a segment rather than an infinite line. This accuracy earns the second mark.

highlights.text: Draw the linear graph of $y = 2x + 5$ with correct endpoints at $(-3, -1)$ and $(-1, 3)$.

steps.title: Identify the transformations
steps.content:
To find the graph of $g(x)$, we analyze how $g(x) = f(x+3) - 2$ transforms the original function $f(x)$. 

From our study of **Topic 2: AHL 2.8 (Transformations of graphs)**, we know:
- Adding a constant inside the function argument, $f(x + h)$, results in a **horizontal translation**. Since we have $+3$, the graph moves **3 units to the left**.
- Subtracting a constant outside the function, $f(x) + k$, results in a **vertical translation**. Since we have $-2$, the graph moves **2 units down**.

So, every point $(x, y)$ on the graph of $f$ is mapped to a new point $(x - 3, y - 2)$ on the graph of $g$.

steps.title: Determine original points
steps.content:
We need to draw $g(x)$ for the interval $-3 \leq x \leq -1$. To find where these points came from on the original graph of $f(x)$, we reverse the horizontal shift.

- The $x = -3$ on $g$ corresponds to $x = 0$ on $f$ (because $0 - 3 = -3$).
- The $x = -1$ on $g$ corresponds to $x = 2$ on $f$ (because $2 - 3 = -1$).

Now, let's find the $y$-coordinates for $f(x) = 2x + 1$ at these points:
- For $x = 0$: $f(0) = 2(0) + 1 = 1$
- For $x = 2$: $f(2) = 2(2) + 1 = 5$

The original points on $f(x)$ are $(0, 1)$ and $(2, 5)$.

steps.title: Apply translation to points
steps.content:
Now we apply the geometric transformations (shift left 3, shift down 2) to our points from $f(x)$ to find the new endpoints for $g(x)$:

$$\begin{aligned} (0, 1) &\xrightarrow{\text{left 3, down 2}} (0 - 3, 1 - 2) = (-3, -1) \\ (2, 5) &\xrightarrow{\text{left 3, down 2}} (2 - 3, 5 - 2) = (-1, 3) \end{aligned}$$

These two points, $(-3, -1)$ and $(-1, 3)$, are the endpoints of the line segment for $g(x)$.

steps.title: Verify and Draw
steps.content:
To draw the graph, plot the points $(-3, -1)$ and $(-1, 3)$ on your axes and connect them with a straight line. 

You can also verify the equation of the line by substituting $f(x+3)$ into the definition of $g(x)$:

$$g(x) = [2(x+3) + 1] - 2 = 2x + 6 + 1 - 2 = 2x + 5$$

Checking our points with $y = 2x + 5$:

- At $x = -3$: $y = 2(-3) + 5 = -1$ (Matches!)
- At $x = -1$: $y = 2(-1) + 5 = 3$ (Matches!)

steps.title: Simplify the composite function
steps.content:
To draw the graph, we first need to find the simplified expression for $g(x)$. We are given $f(x) = 2x + 1$ and $g(x) = f(x+3) - 2$. 

We substitute $(x+3)$ into the function $f$ and then subtract $2$:

$$\begin{aligned} g(x) &= [2(x+3) + 1] - 2 \\ g(x) &= 2x + 6 + 1 - 2 \\ g(x) &= 2x + 5 \end{aligned}$$

This shows that $g(x)$ is a linear function (a straight line).

steps.title: Evaluate at boundary points
steps.content:
Since $g(x)$ is a straight line, we only need to find the coordinates of the endpoints for the given interval $-3 \leq x \leq -1$. Let's evaluate $g(x)$ at these boundaries:

- **Lower boundary ($x = -3$):**

$$g(-3) = 2(-3) + 5 = -6 + 5 = -1$$

This gives us the point $(-3, -1)$.

- **Upper boundary ($x = -1$):**

$$g(-1) = 2(-1) + 5 = -2 + 5 = 3$$

This gives us the point $(-1, 3)$.

steps.title: Draw the line segment
steps.content:
Now, plot the two points we found: $(-3, -1)$ and $(-1, 3)$. 

Connect these points with a solid straight line using a ruler. Ensure that the line **starts exactly** at $x = -3$ and **ends exactly** at $x = -1$ to satisfy the domain restriction. 

In the IB markscheme, you earn one mark for the correct algebraic simplification and one mark for correctly plotting these endpoints and drawing the line.

steps.title: Identify original domain and range
steps.content:
To apply transformations algebraically, we first need to identify the domain and range of the base function $y=f(x)$ from the provided graph.

*   **Domain:** The graph starts at $x=0$ and ends at $x=4$. So, the original domain is $0 \leq x \leq 4$.
*   **Range:** The lowest $y$-value on the graph is $0$ (at the $x$-intercepts) and the highest $y$-value is $1$ (the peak). So, the original range is $0 \leq y \leq 1$.

steps.title: Map domain for 3f(-x)
steps.content:
To find the new domain of $y = 3f(-x)$, we look at the horizontal transformation inside the function. Here, the input is $-x$.

We set the new input equal to the original domain boundaries:
$$0 \leq -x \leq 4$$

To solve for $x$, we multiply the entire inequality by $-1$. Remember that multiplying by a negative number flips the inequality signs:
$$\begin{aligned} 0 &\geq x \geq -4 \\ -4 &\leq x \leq 0 \end{aligned}$$

So, the domain of $y = 3f(-x)$ is $-4 \leq x \leq 0$.

highlights.text: domain and range of y=3 f(-x)

steps.title: Map range for 3f(-x)
steps.content:
The range is affected by vertical transformations outside the function. For $y = 3f(-x)$, the function values are multiplied by $3$.

We take our original range, $0 \leq f(x) \leq 1$, and apply the vertical scale factor:
$$\begin{aligned} 3(0) &\leq 3f(-x) \leq 3(1) \\ 0 &\leq y \leq 3 \end{aligned}$$

The range of $y = 3f(-x)$ is $0 \leq y \leq 3$.

Now let's look at $y = f(2x)$. The inner transformation is $2x$. 

We map this to the original domain boundaries:
$$0 \leq 2x \leq 4$$

Divide the entire inequality by $2$ to solve for $x$:
$$\begin{aligned} \frac{0}{2} &\leq x \leq \frac{4}{2} \\ 0 &\leq x \leq 2 \end{aligned}$$

The domain of $y = f(2x)$ is $0 \leq x \leq 2$.

The original range was $0 \leq y \leq 1$, so the range of $y = f(2x)$ is still $0 \leq y \leq 1$.

steps.title: Analyze the original function
steps.content:
First, let's identify the domain and range of the original function $f(x)$ from the question and the provided graph. 

*   **Original Domain**: The question specifies that $0 \leq x \leq 4$.
*   **Original Range**: By looking at the graph, the $y$-values start at $0$ and reach a maximum height of $1$. So, the range is $0 \leq y \leq 1$.

We will use these base boundaries to determine how the transformations shift the graph.

steps.title: Transformations of 3f(-x)
steps.content:
For the function $y = 3f(-x)$, we have two transformations occurring:

1.  **Reflection in the $y$-axis ($x \to -x$):** This affects the domain. The original $x$-values are multiplied by $-1$. 
    $$\text{New Domain: } 0 \cdot (-1) \geq x \geq 4 \cdot (-1) \Rightarrow -4 \leq x \leq 0$$

2.  **Vertical stretch by a factor of 3 ($y \to 3y$):** This affects the range. The original $y$-values are multiplied by $3$.
    $$\text{New Range: } 0 \cdot 3 \leq y \leq 1 \cdot 3 \Rightarrow 0 \leq y \leq 3$$

highlights.text: Domain of y=3 f(-x) is -4 \leq x \leq 0
Range of y=3 f(-x) is 0 \leq y \leq 3

steps.title: Transformations of f(2x)
steps.content:
Now let's look at $y = f(2x)$. This function involves one horizontal transformation:

1.  **Horizontal compression by a factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ ($x \to \frac{x}{2}$):** Since the $x$ is multiplied by $2$ inside the function, the graph is compressed toward the $y$-axis. We divide the original domain boundaries by $2$.
    $$\text{New Domain: } \frac{0}{2} \leq x \leq \frac{4}{2} \Rightarrow 0 \leq x \leq 2$$

2.  **Vertical changes:** There are no vertical transformations (nothing is multiplying the outside of the function), so the range remains the same as the original.
    $$\text{New Range: } 0 \leq y \leq 1$$

highlights.text: Domain of y=f(2 x) is 0 \leq x \leq 2
Range of y=f(2 x) is 0 \leq y \leq 1

steps.title: Final answer summary
steps.content:
In IB exams, it is important to state your final boundaries clearly. Based on our transformations:

**For $y = 3f(-x)$:**
*   Domain: $-4 \leq x \leq 0$
*   Range: $0 \leq y \leq 3$

**For $y = f(2x)$:**
*   Domain: $0 \leq x \leq 2$
*   Range: $0 \leq y \leq 1$

steps.title: Identify original key points
steps.content:
To use the coordinate transformation approach, we first look at the original graph of $y = f(x)$ to identify the start and end points of the function. 

From the graph, the function begins at the origin $(0, 0)$ and ends at the point $(4, 1)$. 

This means the original **domain** is $0 \leq x \leq 4$ and the original **range** is $0 \leq y \leq 1$.

steps.title: Transform points for 3f(-x)
steps.content:
For the function $y = 3f(-x)$, there are two transformations happening to the original coordinates $(x, y)$:
1. The $-x$ inside the function reflects the graph across the $y$-axis, changing $x$ to $-x$.
2. The $3$ outside the function is a vertical stretch, multiplying $y$ by $3$.

Let's apply these rules to our key points:
$$\begin{aligned} (0, 0) &\rightarrow (-(0), 3 \times 0) = (0, 0) \\ (4, 1) &\rightarrow (-(4), 3 \times 1) = (-4, 3) \end{aligned}$$

The new $x$-values are between $-4$ and $0$, and the new $y$-values are between $0$ and $3$.

steps.title: State first domain and range
steps.content:
Based on our transformed points $(0,0)$ and $(-4, 3)$, we can write down the results for the first part of the question:

*   **Domain of $y=3f(-x)$:** $-4 \leq x \leq 0$
*   **Range of $y=3f(-x)$:** $0 \leq y \leq 3$

steps.title: Transform points for f(2x)
steps.content:
Now let's look at the function $y = f(2x)$. The $2$ is inside the function multiplying the $x$, which represents a horizontal compression. 

To find the new $x$-coordinates, we divide the original $x$-values by $2$ (or multiply by $\frac{1}{2}$). The $y$-coordinates remain unchanged since there is no transformation outside the function.

Applying this to our key points:
$$\begin{aligned} (0, 0) &\rightarrow (0 \div 2, 0) = (0, 0) \\ (4, 1) &\rightarrow (4 \div 2, 1) = (2, 1) \end{aligned}$$

The new $x$-values are between $0$ and $2$, and the $y$-values stay between $0$ and $1$.

steps.title: State final domain and range
steps.content:
Using the transformed points $(0,0)$ and $(2, 1)$, we can state the final set of answers:

*   **Domain of $y=f(2x)$:** $0 \leq x \leq 2$
*   **Range of $y=f(2x)$:** $0 \leq y \leq 1$

This coordinate method is a great way to double-check your work on transformation questions!

steps.title: Identify the transformation
steps.content:
The question asks us to sketch $y = -f(x)$. In function transformations, when we multiply the entire function by $-1$, it results in a **reflection in the $x$-axis**. 

Mathematically, this means that for every point $(x, y)$ on the original graph, the new point on the transformed graph will be $(x, -y)$. This is the core of the **Coordinate Mapping** approach.

steps.title: Locate key original coordinates
steps.content:
Let's identify some clear points (intercepts and local extrema) from the given graph of $f(x)$:

*   **$x$-intercepts**: $(-3, 0)$, $(-1, 0)$, and $(2, 0)$
*   **Local maximum**: $(-2, 2)$
*   **Local minimum**: $(1, -2)$
*   **$y$-intercept**: $(0, -1)$

steps.title: Negate the y-values
steps.content:
Using the mapping rule $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, -y)$, we transform our key points:

$$\begin{aligned} (-3, 0) &\rightarrow (-3, 0) \\ (-2, 2) &\rightarrow (-2, -2) \\ (-1, 0) &\rightarrow (-1, 0) \\ (0, -1) &\rightarrow (0, 1) \\ (1, -2) &\rightarrow (1, 2) \\ (2, 0) &\rightarrow (2, 0) \end{aligned}$$

Notice that the $x$-intercepts remain unchanged because negating zero still results in zero.

steps.title: Sketch the reflected graph
steps.content:
Now, plot these new points on the grid and connect them with a smooth curve. 

*   The original peak at $(-2, 2)$ becomes a valley at $(-2, -2)$.
*   The original valley at $(1, -2)$ becomes a peak at $(1, 2)$.

The resulting curve is the mirror image of $f(x)$ across the horizontal $x$-axis.

parts.content: Describe fully the transformation that maps the graph of $f$ to the graph of $g$.
parts.markscheme:
translation by the vector $\begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$
<marks>A1</marks>

**Note:** Accept "translation 3 units to the left". Do not accept "shift" or "slide" as the primary term, though they may be accepted as synonyms if the vector is correct.

steps.title: Analyze the function form
steps.content:
To identify the transformation, we look at how the function $f$ has been modified to create $g$. The question gives us the relationship:

$$g(x) = f(x+3)$$

This fits the general functional form $y = f(x + h)$, where a constant $h$ is added directly to the input variable $x$ before the function is applied.

steps.title: Apply the translation rule
steps.content:
In coordinate geometry, adding a constant to the input $x$ results in a **horizontal translation**. The standard algebraic rule is:

- If $h > 0$, the graph moves to the **left**.
- If $h < 0$, the graph moves to the **right**.

Since we have $+3$, the value of $h$ is positive, meaning the entire graph of $f$ is shifted 3 units in the negative $x$-direction.

steps.title: Describe the transformation fully
steps.content:
In IB exams, it is best to use the term **translation** and provide either the direction or a translation vector. A movement of 3 units to the left with no vertical movement is represented by the vector:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

According to the markscheme, the full description is a **translation by the vector $\begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$** or **translation 3 units to the left**. 

*Note: Avoid using informal words like "shift" or "move" as your primary answer; "translation" is the required mathematical term.*

steps.title: Understand coordinate mapping
steps.content:
To find how the graph changes from $f$ to $g$, we look at the relation $g(x) = f(x+3)$. 

For $g(x)$ to produce the exact same $y$-value that $f$ produced at some point $x_0$, the input of $f$ must be $x_0$. In our equation, this means:
$$x + 3 = x_0$$

Solving for the 'new' $x$ coordinate of $g$:
$$x = x_0 - 3$$

This tells us that every point $(x_0, y)$ on the graph of $f$ is mapped to a new point $(x_0 - 3, y)$ on the graph of $g$.

steps.title: Map a specific point
steps.content:
Let's pick an identifiable point on the graph of $f$. Looking at the diagram, there is a local maximum (a peak) at approximately $x = -2$.

Using our mapping rule $x = x_0 - 3$:
$$\text{New } x = -2 - 3 = -5$$

The peak that was at $x = -2$ on $f$ will now be at $x = -5$ on $g$. Because the $x$-coordinate decreased by $3$, the entire graph has moved $3$ units in the negative $x$-direction (to the left).

steps.title: Verify with another point
steps.content:
Let's check the $y$-intercept of $f$, which is at $x = 0$.

Using the rule $x = x_0 - 3$:
$$\text{New } x = 0 - 3 = -3$$

The point that was at $(0, y)$ on $f$ is now at $(-3, y)$ on $g$. This confirms that every single point on the graph is being shifted $3$ units to the left.

steps.title: State the full transformation
steps.content:
In IB Mathematics, we must use the precise term **translation** to describe this movement. A horizontal shift of $3$ units to the left with no vertical movement is written as a translation by the vector:

$$\begin{pmatrix} -3 \\ 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

This covers the 'full description' required for the mark.

parts.content:
The graph of the function $f(x) = x^3$ is reflected in the $y$-axis and then translated by the vector $\begin{pmatrix} a \\ b \end{pmatrix}$ to form the graph of the function $g$.

The graph of $g$ passes through the points $(1, 11)$ and $(4, -16)$.

Find the value of $a$ and the value of $b$, given that $a > 2$.
parts.markscheme:
- State the form of the transformed function $g$: 
  $g(x) = f(-(x - a)) + b$
  $g(x) = (a - x)^3 + b$ (or $g(x) = -(x - a)^3 + b$) <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute the point $(1, 11)$ into the function:
  $11 = (a - 1)^3 + b$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Substitute the point $(4, -16)$ into the function:
  $-16 = (a - 4)^3 + b$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Attempt to solve the system of equations by subtracting the second from the first:
  $11 - (-16) = (a - 1)^3 - (a - 4)^3$
  $27 = (a - 1)^3 - (a - 4)^3$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Expand and simplify to a quadratic equation (or use substitution $u = a - 4$):
  $27 = (a^3 - 3a^2 + 3a - 1) - (a^3 - 12a^2 + 48a - 64)$
  $27 = 9a^2 - 45a + 63$
  $9a^2 - 45a + 36 = 0$
  $a^2 - 5a + 4 = 0$ 
  $(a - 4)(a - 1) = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Select $a = 4$ (since $a > 2$) and find $b$:
  $b = -16 - (4 - 4)^3 = -16$
  $a = 4, b = -16$ <marks>A1</marks>

6 marks total

steps.title: Identify the base function
steps.content:
The original behavior of the pendulum is modeled by the cosine function:

$$\displaystyle f(x) = \cos(x)$$

where $x$ represents the time in seconds. Our goal is to modify this function to represent a horizontal shrink.

steps.title: Apply the transformation rule
steps.content:
To perform a **horizontal shrink** by a factor of $k$, we use the algebraic rule of replacing the input variable $x$ with $kx$.

In this case, we want a shrink by a factor of 4. This means we replace $x$ with $4x$ inside the function argument. This concept is covered in **Topic 2: Functions** (specifically AHL 2.8) of your syllabus.

highlights.text: horizontal shrink by a factor of 4

steps.title: Write the final equation
steps.content:
By substituting $4x$ for $x$ in our original function, we get the new function, which we can call $h(x)$:

$$\displaystyle h(x) = \cos(4x)$$

This transformation "speeds up" the oscillations, making them happen 4 times faster than the original pendulum.

steps.title: Identify the original period
steps.content:
To use the Period Adjustment Method, we first need to identify the period of the original function. The function $f(x) = \cos(x)$ is a standard trigonometric function. 

The standard period ($P$) for a cosine function $y = \cos(x)$ is $2\pi$. This means the pendulum completes one full oscillation every $2\pi$ seconds.

steps.title: Calculate the new period
steps.content:
The question specifies a **horizontal shrink by a factor of 4**. In terms of the period, a horizontal shrink means the oscillation happens faster, so the period decreases.

We calculate the new period ($P_{new}$) by dividing the original period by the shrink factor:

$$\displaystyle P_{new} = \frac{2\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2 * pi / 4

ti84.instructions: Enter 2 * π / 4

desmos.instructions: Type 2pi / 4

calculator.description: Verify the calculation of the new period.

highlights.text: horizontal shrink by a factor of 4

steps.title: Find the frequency coefficient
steps.content:
In the general form $y = \cos(bx)$, the coefficient $b$ represents the frequency factor. The relationship between the period ($P$) and $b$ is:

$$\displaystyle P = \frac{2\pi}{b}$$

Rearranging this to solve for $b$:

$$\displaystyle b = \frac{2\pi}{P_{new}}$$

Substituting our new period:

$$\displaystyle \begin{aligned} b &= \frac{2\pi}{\frac{\pi}{2}} \\ b &= 2\pi \cdot \frac{2}{\pi} \\ b &= 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Write the final equation
steps.content:
Now that we have the frequency coefficient $b = 4$, we substitute it back into the general cosine function form to get our transformed function, $h(x)$.

$$\displaystyle h(x) = \cos(4x)$$

This matches the markscheme requirement of substituting $4x$ for $x$.

steps.title: Identify original period
steps.content: The original function is $f(x) = \cos(x)$. For any standard cosine function, the period—which is the horizontal distance required for the function to complete one full cycle—is $2\pi$ radians.

steps.title: Analyze horizontal shrink
steps.content:
A horizontal shrink by a factor of 4 means the function completes its cycles four times faster than the original. In terms of the period, we divide the original period by the shrink factor:

$$\displaystyle \text{New Period} = \frac{2\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter 2 * π / 4.

desmos.instructions: Type 2π / 4 in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of 2π divided by 4.

highlights.text: Identify the effect of horizontal shrink on period: \frac{2\pi}{4} = \frac{\pi}{2}

steps.title: Consider vertical stretch
steps.content: A vertical stretch changes the height of the graph (the amplitude), but it does not compress or expand the graph horizontally. Therefore, the vertical stretch has no effect on the period of the function.

highlights.text: Recognize that vertical stretch does not affect the period

steps.title: State the new period
steps.content:
By combining these observations, we conclude that only the horizontal transformation altered the period. The new period of the function is:

$$\displaystyle \text{Period} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

steps.title: Identify the General Form
steps.content:
A transformed cosine function can be expressed in the general form:

$$\displaystyle f(x) = a \cos(bx)$$

In this form, the constant $a$ represents the amplitude (vertical stretch), and the constant $b$ is the horizontal frequency coefficient, which determines the period of the function.

steps.title: Determine the Value of b
steps.content:
According to the problem, the function undergoes a horizontal shrink by a factor of $4$. In the general equation $f(x) = a \cos(bx)$, this means the horizontal frequency coefficient is:

$$\displaystyle b = 4$$

highlights.text: identify the horizontal frequency coefficient b as 4

steps.title: Evaluate the Vertical Stretch
steps.content: The function also undergoes a vertical stretch. However, the period of a trigonometric function is only affected by horizontal transformations. Vertical stretches change the maximum and minimum values (the amplitude) but do not change the time it takes for one full oscillation.

highlights.text: Recognize that vertical stretch does not affect the period

steps.title: Apply the Period Formula
steps.content:
The period $T$ of a transformed cosine function is found using the formula:

$$\displaystyle T = \frac{2\pi}{b}$$

Substituting our value of $b = 4$:

$$\displaystyle \begin{aligned} T &= \frac{2\pi}{4} \\ T &= \frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (2 * π) / 4 and press EXE.

desmos.instructions: Type (2 * pi) / 4 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the period using the formula T = 2π/b where b=4.

highlights.text: substitute this into the standard period formula T = 2π/b

steps.title: State the New Period
steps.content: The new period of the function after the transformations is $\frac{\pi}{2}$ seconds.

parts.content:
Let $k(x) = \sin(x)$

Describe the transformation applied to $k(x)$ to obtain
$m(x) = 4\sin(2x)$
parts.markscheme:
- Vertical stretch by a factor of 4 <marks>A1</marks>
- Horizontal stretch by a scale factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the transformation form
steps.content:
To describe the transformation, we compare the given function $m(x) = 4\sin(2x)$ to the original function $k(x) = \sin(x)$. 

We can write this in the general transformation form:
$$m(x) = a \cdot k(bx)$$

By comparing $4\sin(2x)$ to $a \cdot \sin(bx)$, we can identify the constants:
- $a = 4$
- $b = 2$

steps.title: Analyze the vertical transformation
steps.content:
The constant $a$ is multiplying the entire function. In function notation, $y = a \cdot f(x)$ represents a **vertical stretch**.

Since $a = 4$, the graph is stretched vertically by a factor of $4$. This means every $y$-coordinate of the original sine wave is multiplied by $4$, increasing the amplitude.

highlights.text: Vertical stretch by a factor of 4

steps.title: Analyze the horizontal transformation
steps.content:
The constant $b$ is multiplying the input $x$. In function notation, $y = f(bx)$ represents a **horizontal stretch**.

The scale factor for a horizontal stretch is always the reciprocal of $b$, which is $\frac{1}{b}$.

Given $b = 2$, we calculate the scale factor:
$$\text{Horizontal scale factor} = \frac{1}{2}$$

This means the graph is compressed horizontally, making the wave repeat twice as often.

highlights.text: Horizontal stretch by a scale factor of $\frac{1}{2}$

steps.content: It's often helpful to graph both functions to see these transformations in action. You will notice $m(x)$ is much taller (vertical stretch) and more "squished" (horizontal compression) than $k(x)$.

steps.title: Final transformation description
steps.content:
Combining our findings, the two transformations are:
1. A **vertical stretch** by a factor of $4$.
2. A **horizontal stretch** by a scale factor of $\frac{1}{2}$.

Note: In the IB markscheme, mentioning both the type of transformation (stretch) and the correct scale factor is required for full marks.

steps.title: Analyze the vertical change
steps.content:
To describe the transformations, we first compare the **amplitude** of the two functions. 

The original function $k(x) = \sin(x)$ has an amplitude of $1$. 

The transformed function $m(x) = 4\sin(2x)$ has a leading coefficient of $4$. This means the maximum and minimum values are now $4$ times further from the x-axis, increasing the amplitude to $4$.

A change in amplitude represents a **vertical stretch**. Since the value is multiplied by $4$, the scale factor is $4$.

highlights.text: Vertical stretch by a factor of 4

steps.title: Analyze the horizontal change
steps.content:
Next, we look at the **period** of the functions by examining the coefficient inside the sine function. 

The original function $k(x) = \sin(x)$ has a period of $2\pi$. 

For $m(x) = 4\sin(2x)$, the coefficient of $x$ (often called $b$) is $2$. We can find the new period using the relationship:

$$\text{New Period} = \frac{2\pi}{b} = \frac{2\pi}{2} = \pi$$

Comparing the periods, the wave now completes a full cycle in half the distance ($2\pi \rightarrow \pi$). This represents a **horizontal stretch** (or compression) by a scale factor of $\frac{1}{2}$.

steps.title: Identify the original function
steps.content:
The original waveform is modeled by the function:

$$f(x) = \sin(x)$$

This represents a standard sine wave with an amplitude of 1. To modify this signal, we need to apply a transformation based on the engineer's requirements.

steps.title: Apply vertical stretch rule
steps.content:
To perform a vertical stretch, we apply the algebraic transformation rule for functions. If we want to stretch a function $f(x)$ vertically by a scale factor of $a$, the new function $g(x)$ is defined as:

$$g(x) = a \cdot f(x)$$

In this problem, the scale factor is 13. This transformation is covered in **Topic 2: Functions** (specifically AHL 2.8) of your syllabus.

highlights.text: vertical stretch by a factor of 13

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the value $a = 13$ and the original function $f(x) = \sin(x)$ into our transformation rule:

$$\begin{aligned} g(x) &= 13 \cdot f(x) \\ g(x) &= 13 \cdot \sin(x) \\ g(x) &= 13\sin(x) \end{aligned}$$

This results in a sine wave where the maximum displacement (amplitude) has increased from 1 to 13.

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: You can verify this transformation by graphing both functions on your calculator. You will notice that the peaks of the new graph reach $y = 13$ instead of $y = 1$.

steps.title: Identify original function
steps.content: The sound wave is originally modeled by the function $f(x) = \sin(x)$. We are asked to determine the equation of this function after it undergoes a specific vertical transformation.

steps.title: Link stretch to amplitude
steps.content:
In trigonometry, a vertical stretch of a function $f(x)$ by a factor of $k$ is represented by the transformation $k \cdot f(x)$. For a basic sine wave, this stretch factor corresponds directly to the **amplitude** (the maximum displacement from the horizontal axis). 

This transformation is a key part of modeling functions, as seen in **Topic 2: Functions** (SL 2.5 and AHL 2.8).

steps.title: Apply the stretch factor
steps.content:
The problem specifies a vertical stretch by a factor of 13. To find the new function, we multiply the original sine function by 13:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New Function} &= 13 \cdot f(x) \\ &= 13 \cdot \sin(x) \\ &= 13\sin(x) \end{aligned}$$

This stretch means the sound wave now reaches a maximum height of 13 and a minimum of -13.

steps.title: Start with fundamental range
steps.content:
To find the range of a transformed sinusoidal function using **Analytical Range Mapping**, we begin with the fundamental range of the parent function, $f(x) = \sin(x)$. 

The sine function always oscillates between $-1$ and $1$. We can write this as:
$$-1 \leq \sin(x) \leq 1$$

steps.title: Identify the amplitude
steps.content:
The range of a trigonometric function is determined by its vertical transformations. From the problem context (and as noted in the markscheme), the transformed function has a specific amplitude.

Horizontal transformations (like frequency changes) do not affect the $y$-values (the range), so we focus on the vertical stretch factor, which is the amplitude.

steps.title: Map the boundaries
steps.content:
Now, we apply the vertical stretch factor (amplitude of 13) to our fundamental range. We multiply both the minimum and maximum values of the original range by 13:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{New Minimum} &= -1 \times 13 = -13 \\ \text{New Maximum} &= 1 \times 13 = 13 \end{aligned}$$

Since there is no vertical shift mentioned in the transformations, the center of the wave remains at $y = 0$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate -1 * 13 and 1 * 13 on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Simply multiply the values on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the products in separate rows.

calculator.description: Calculate the range boundaries by multiplying the unit circle extremes by the amplitude.

steps.title: State the final range
steps.content:
By mapping the original interval $[-1, 1]$ using the stretch factor, we find that the $y$-values now span from $-13$ to $13$.

In IB notation, we can express the range as an interval or an inequality:
$$[-13, 13] \quad \text{or} \quad -13 \leq y \leq 13$$

steps.title: Identify the transformed function
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the problem, we know that the sound wave's amplitude has been transformed. The new amplitude is $13$. Since the original function was $f(x) = \sin(x)$, the transformed function involving a change in amplitude and frequency can be written as:

$$g(x) = 13 \sin(bx)$$

where $b$ represents the horizontal transformation (frequency). In finding the range, we focus on the vertical extent of the graph.

steps.title: Graph the function
steps.content:
To visualize the range, we can plot the function on a graphing display calculator (GDC). We will use the function $y = 13 \sin(x)$ to determine the maximum and minimum heights of the wave. 

When graphing, ensure your window settings are adjusted so that the peaks and troughs are visible. For example, set your $y$-axis range from $-15$ to $15$.

3. Use the 'G-Solv' tool (F5) and select 'MAX' to find the highest point and 'MIN' to find the lowest point.

2. Click on the gray points at the peaks and troughs of the curve to see their coordinates.

steps.title: Determine the range
steps.content:
By analyzing the graph, we can see that the highest point (global maximum) occurs at $y = 13$ and the lowest point (global minimum) occurs at $y = -13$. 

The range represents all possible $y$-values the function can take. Since the wave oscillates continuously between these two values, the range is the interval from $-13$ to $13$.

steps.content:
We express the range using interval notation or as an inequality. 

$$\text{Range: } [-13, 13]$$
$$\text{or } -13 \leq y \leq 13$$
